complexityAnalysis.expansionPrompt: Break down the infrastructure provisioning into discrete subtasks for each data store and supporting service. Each subtask should focus on deploying a single component (PostgreSQL, Redis/Valkey, Kafka, MongoDB, RabbitMQ, SeaweedFS, ConfigMaps, and Secrets) with its operator configuration, resource allocation, and connectivity validation. Include specific kubectl commands, operator CR definitions, and verification steps for each component.
complexityAnalysis.reasoning: This task has high complexity due to: (1) Multiple heterogeneous data stores requiring different operators and configurations, (2) Complex inter-dependencies between services that must be orchestrated correctly, (3) Storage provisioning with mayastor requiring careful resource management, (4) Operator-specific configurations (CloudNative-PG, Strimzi, Percona, RabbitMQ) each with unique CRDs, (5) Network connectivity validation across namespaces, (6) Security considerations for credentials and secrets management. While the steps are well-documented, the coordination of 6+ different operators and ensuring all connection strings are properly configured adds significant implementation risk. Testing requires validating each service independently and then verifying inter-service connectivity.

complexityAnalysis.expansionPrompt: Decompose the Rust service implementation into focused subtasks covering: (1) Project initialization and dependency setup, (2) Data model definitions with validation, (3) PostgreSQL integration with sqlx, (4) Redis rate limiting implementation, (5) Kafka producer setup and event publishing, (6) Deduplication logic, (7) Axum route handlers and middleware, (8) WebSocket real-time notification system, (9) Prometheus metrics instrumentation, (10) Health check endpoints, (11) Containerization and Kubernetes deployment. Each subtask should include specific code implementations, error handling patterns, and unit test requirements.
complexityAnalysis.reasoning: This task scores very high in complexity due to: (1) Multiple concurrent I/O operations requiring careful async/await orchestration with Tokio, (2) Integration with 3 different data stores (PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka) each with distinct client libraries and error handling, (3) Real-time WebSocket implementation requiring connection management and broadcasting, (4) Rate limiting and deduplication requiring atomic operations and race condition handling, (5) High-performance requirements (10k notifications/min, <100ms p95 latency) demanding optimization, (6) Prometheus metrics requiring careful instrumentation without performance impact, (7) Complex error handling across multiple failure domains, (8) Multi-stage Docker builds for Rust optimization. The combination of performance requirements, multiple integration points, and real-time features makes this one of the most technically challenging tasks.

complexityAnalysis.taskTitle: Implement Integration Service with Effect (Nova - Bun/Elysia)
complexityAnalysis.expansionPrompt: Break down the Effect TypeScript service into subtasks covering: (1) Project setup with Bun and Effect dependencies, (2) Effect Schema definitions for all domain models and error types, (3) Slack service implementation with Effect retry logic, (4) Discord service implementation, (5) Email service with SMTP integration, (6) Webhook service with signature verification, (7) MongoDB repository layer with Effect error handling, (8) Kafka consumer using Effect Stream, (9) RabbitMQ task queue integration, (10) Elysia REST API routes, (11) Effect Layer composition and dependency injection, (12) Containerization and deployment. Each subtask should demonstrate proper Effect patterns including error handling, retry policies, and resource management.
complexityAnalysis.reasoning: This task has very high complexity due to: (1) Effect TypeScript's advanced type system and functional programming paradigm requiring deep expertise, (2) Four different external integration channels (Slack, Discord, Email, Webhook) each with unique APIs and error modes, (3) Effect Stream for Kafka consumption requiring backpressure management and error recovery, (4) Complex retry logic with exponential backoff and jittered schedules, (5) Effect Schema validation for multiple configuration types, (6) MongoDB integration with Effect's error handling model, (7) Effect Layer composition for dependency injection across services, (8) Concurrent message processing with Effect's structured concurrency, (9) Rate limiting coordination across channels, (10) Template rendering with Handlebars for email/webhook payloads. The combination of Effect's learning curve, multiple external integrations, and sophisticated error handling patterns makes this extremely challenging.
